reasoning: The claimed mechanism — that paged-based pagination causes offset skipping and incomplete erasure — does not occur in this code. Both Flamingo_Contact::find() and Flamingo_Inbound_Message::find() include 'offset' => 0 in their $defaults array (class-contact.php line 41; class-inbound-message.php line 61). When the eraser calls find() with 'paged' => $page, wp_parse_args() merges caller args over defaults, producing an $args array containing BOTH 'offset' => 0 AND 'paged' => $page. Both are passed verbatim to WP_Query::query(). WordPress core WP_Query behavior: when 'offset' is explicitly present in the query args (even as 0), it overrides pagination derived from 'paged' — WP_Query uses the literal offset value and ignores paged. As a result, every invocation of the eraser — regardless of $page — queries with LIMIT 100 OFFSET 0, i.e., always from the beginning of remaining records. Because page 1 deletes records 1-100, page 2 queries offset 0 again and finds the next 100 (formerly 101-200, now at position 1-100). Deletion is complete; records are not skipped. The done condition (Flamingo_Contact::count() < $number at line 66; Flamingo_Inbound_Message::count() < $number at line 112) uses self::$found_items from the last find() call, which causes an extra unnecessary final round-trip when the last page contains exactly 100 records, but this does not leave any records undeleted. The finding's specific claim — 'page 2 skips them entirely and the eraser silently terminates with done=true, leaving up to N-1 batches of personal data undeleted' — is factually incorrect for this code. The 'paged' argument is a no-op here, not a source of data loss. No GDPR erasure incompleteness exists via the described mechanism. Reachability analysis is correct (WP core handles auth for wp_ajax_wp_privacy_erase_personal_data), but irrelevant since the underlying defect does not exist.
